77.title: Guidelines for Character Creation
77.content: In Chime, you’ll play a Crew of prisoners who have accepted a Debt-Curse in exchange for some form of freedom, lugging treasures for the Martel Trading Company on Hot Springs Island. The following assumptions apply to your character, all else being fair game for you to determine: . | You all have come to live for some time in your home region’s single dominant metropolis, known simply as “The City”. The setting is defined minimally via your various Failed Careers, and the evocative art associated with those careers, and our campaign will mainly take place out of The City. But this List of principles might help give you a feel for where your character is from. | Each Character has tried to pursue a normal career, as detailed by their Failed Career, and utterly failed at that career in some way. | Each Character has spent time imprisoned, whether guilty or innocent. | Your Characters are bound to the rest of the Crew by an accursed bargain with the Martel Trading Company, taking on a Collective Debt in the form of a curse which eats away at your body unless treasure is regularly hauled off Hot Springs Island. | .

78.title: Creating a Character:
78.content: . | Print Out a Character Sheet, or doodle your own on a sheet of paper. Your Character Sheet needs space for your Character’s Name, Attributes, Hit Protection, Currency, a Failed Career, and 10 Inventory Slots. Leave space to add other details about your Character and their relationships to others in the crew. Please do have a paper copy of your character sheet when playing the game rather than using the PDF - it’ll free up your screen and keep you more engaged during play. | Roll 3d6 for the three Attributes, STRength, DEXterity, WILLpower, in order. These Attributes represent your Character’s ability to handle themselves in risky, split-second moments, but not their overall abilities. After you have rolled, you may then pick two of the results and swap them, if you’d like. | Characters start with d6 Hit Protection (HP). Hit Protection represents a Character’s ability to avoid taking serious damage. | Characters start with d6 × 2 New Pounds (£), enough to buy a couple sandwiches of varying quality. | You had a shot at a normal life and you blew it. Match your highest Attribute with your lowest to determine your Failed Career. There are two tables associated with further features related to your failed career; matching your Hit Protection and Starting Currency to each table determines which entries are associated with your character. | Choose a Name for your character. Here’s some tips: . | Some folks have one name, others have multiple. | Some have nicknames, aliases, family names, or village names. | Some names come with descripters, sometimes accurate, and sometimes false (Bujamew the Magnanimous, Fragrant Joe, etc). | Some names describe a character’s class (Agu the Money Lender, Bilbo the Burglar, etc). | Take inspiration from the names associated with your Failed Career, or more general Tables of names. | . | Choose some Character Traits to distinguish your character, or Roll on some tables for inspiration. | Randomly choose three other characters from the crew, and form Connections with them. Maybe you knew each other before you both became part of the Crew, or maybe your Connection represents your initial hunches about them? | .

79.title: Forming Connections
79.content: A Connection is what we call the relationships you have with the other members of your Crew. Fleshing out these connections helps make the Crew feel more dynamic, and also adds depth to your character. They can often be summarized as simple statements (“I am Petyr’s Mentor”, “Grendel is my Rival , etc). The Relationship Archetype list can be used to help inspire you.

80.title: Further Character Development
80.content: When a session concludes, answer one of the following questions: . | Why did your character become imprisoned. What prompted them to subject themselves to the bondage contract of the Martel Trading Company? . | What does your character value the most, and what has it cost you so far to maintain that value? . | Think of a time your adventurer struggled with something while they were young, that they are still ashamed of today. | What was the moment that you realized your Failed Career wasn’t, or couldn’t be, right for you? . | Back in The City, there’s someone who’d be disappointed to see you now. Who are they, and why? . | There was a rumor about this island you dismissed back in prison. But now it seems true? What was the rumor, and what does it say about you that you dismissed it? . | . After answering a question, ask yourself whether this answer represents a Core Belief your character might hold. If so, write that down. The answers to these questions can provide inspiration for what your characters will talk about during a short rest. In addition, form a new Connection with a Character from the Session you’ve just played, who you are not yet connected to, and summarize this connection in a sentence. This connection may be related to events between the characters in the present Session, or a past that exists before our campaign started. Connections are not fixed in stone. Review your Connections with the current Session’s Characters, and discuss with the other players if you feel your interactions in game have strengthened, weakened, or fundamentally adjusted the relationship you have with one another. Once you’ve checked off at least three of the questions above, you’ve formed two Core Beliefs, and you’ve formed at least six Connections with other characters (see below), pick an attribute, and roll 3d6. If the total is higher than your current attribute, take the new result.

92.content: Paid a petty fee to occupy a desolate rock, in place of a flag. The years have been dull, so you return to the mainland.

96.title: What Did You Find On The Island?

It knows everything. | . | 2HP | Pack of Fat Beans - Eating one makes you unable to run or jump until you get a night’s sleep. | . | 3HP | Jar of Black Air - Releasing it plunges the room into darkness until fresh light is brought into it. One use only. | . | 4HP | Jar of Unidentified Blood - If poured into a dead body, the body serves you in one task before vomiting up the blood and dying even more. | . | 5HP | Star Atlas - Shows constellations you do not recognize from the sky. Anyone harming this book is struck by lightning (d12, ignore AP). | . | 6HP | Badly Stitched Doll: Terrifies children and animals. | .

97.title: Player Expectations
97.content: These rules provide guidelines for the GM and other Players, to help foster a specific play experience defined by critical thinking, engagement with the imaginary world, character development, and emergent narrative. Roles . Chime challenges the GM to act as a neutral arbiter, portraying grounded situations and non-player characters to the other players, and providing contextual clarity to foster the other Players ability to make interesting decisions. Chime challenges the other Players to engage with the imaginary world, portray interesting characters with strong beliefs, respond to situations in clever and interesting ways, and to drive the fiction forward in interesting ways through their decisions. Fiction First . In world context overrides mechanics and numbers. Players engage with the world by describing their character’s actions within the world, with most consequences of those actions arbitrated by the GM rather than mediated through specific rules. This is closely related to the maxim of ‘Rulings Not Rules’. Classless . A Character’s role or skills is not defined by a particular class or attributes. Instead, a character specializes with their experience, their learning, and the equipment they choose to carry. Danger . Characters may be powerful, but they are also vulnerable to harm in its many forms. Death is always around the corner, but it should never be random or without warning. A dagger to the throat will kill a man, regardless of their expensive armor and impressive training. This danger adds impact to a Player’s decisions and compels them to engage with the imaginary world, because their character is not superheroic enough to stand above it. Growth . Characters are changed through in-world advancement, gaining new skills and abilities by surviving dangerous events, overcoming obstacles, and making connections with others within the imaginary world. Player Choice . Players should always understand the reasons behind the choices they’ve made, and information about potential risks should be provided freely and frequently by the GM. Emergent Narrative . Neither the GM nor the other Players are responsible for molding a narrative structure from the game. Players act according to beliefs, motivations, and their perception of the imaginary world, and the GM provides a lens to the imaginary world, as well as fostering the Player’s ability to make interesting decisions. Narrative emerges from these interesting decisions in retrospect, just as stories emerge in real life, i.e. by filtering through a series of events to find a chain of interconnected events that have meaning to us.

98.title: Player Guidelines
98.content: Agency . | Attributes are tools to engage with the imaginary world, but do not define your character. | Engage with the imaginary world as if you existed within it. | Be creative with your intuitions, items, and connections. Try and make the GM say “I didn’t even think of that!”. | Keep games focused by coming to a game with a plan of what you want to accomplish in a session already discussed with other players. | . Teamwork . | Trust each other to engage with the shared setting, to help others with the goals of their characters and of the overall team. Work together as a team. | Seek consensus from other players before barreling forward. | Stay on the same page about goals and limits, respecting each other and accomplishing more as a group than alone. | Let the relationships between your characters grow and change. | . Engage With The World . | Asking questions, listening to details, and engaging with the world is more useful than any stats, items, or skills you have. | Take the GM’s description without suspicion, but do not shy away from scrutinizing your environment for more information. | There is no correct solution to any problem, nor any correct path forward. | . Talking . | Treat NPCs as if they were real people, and rely on your curiosity to safely gain information and solve problems. | You’ll find that most people are interesting and will want to talk things through before getting violent. | . Caution . | Think of ways to avoid your obstacles through reconnaissance, subtlety, and information gathering. | Fighting is a choice, and rarely a wise one; consider whether violence is the best way to achieve your goals. | Stack the odds in your favor as much as possible, and always keep retreat as an option when a situation becomes unfavorable. | . Ambition . | Set goals for your characters, and use your meager means to accomplish them. | Embody characters with beliefs that distinguish them from the general party. Set personal goals to develop your character further. | Actively move situations forward when they stall, plan goals for sessions in advance to keep games focused. | Play to find out what happens rather than anticipating plot threads or expecting certain outcomes. | .

99.title: GM Guidelines
99.content: Information . | Provide useful information in response to the explorations of the Players. Respond to smart questions with helpful, direct, consistent answers. | Players do not need to roll dice to understand their circumstances. | . Difficulty . | If something the players want to do is sincerely impossible, no roll will allow them to do it. | If a player leverages a situation in a sensible, risk-free manner, let the consequences happen without a roll. | . Preparation . | Consider the Blorb principles. | Use random tables and generators to develop situations, not stories or plots. | NPCs remember what the PCs say and do, and how they affect the world. | NPCs don’t want to die. Infuse their own self-interest and will to live into every personality. | . Narrative Focus . | Emergent play matters more than a planned outcome. | Give the players opportunities to facilitate improvement and specialization, and encourage them to foster personal goals. | Pay attention to the desires and beliefs of the players, then put realistic opportunities in their path. | . Danger . | The game world produces real risk of pain and death for the player characters. | Telegraph serious danger to players when it is present. The more dangerous, the more obvious. Put traps in plain sight. | Give players opportunities to solve problems and interact with the world. | . Treasure . | Treasures are specific to the environment from where it is recovered. They tell a story. | Treasure is highly valuable, almost always Bulky, and rarely useful beyond its worth and prestige. | Relics are not Treasure, though they are useful and interesting. | Use Treasure as a lure to exotic locations protected of intimidating foes. | . Choice . | Give players solid choices to force outcomes when situations lull. | Ensure that the player character’s actions are impactful, leaving a mark on the imaginary world. | .

100.title: Core Rules
100.content: . | Attributes | Hit Protection (HP) | Resolution Systems . | Saves | Time, Gear, & Skill | The Die of Fate | . | Inventory . | Inventory Slots | Conditions, Deprivation, and Fatigue | . | Damage, Injury and Death . | Wounds | Character Death | . | Healing & Recovery . | Catching Your Breathe | Long Term Recovery | . | Magic . | Spellbooks | Casting Spells | . | Combat . | Rounds | Actions | Attacking & Damage | Attack Modifiers | Gambits | Armor Protection (AP) | Detachments | Retreat | Morale | Ranged Attacks | Scars | . | .

101.title: Attributes
101.content: Player Characters (PCs) have three Attributes: . | Strength (STR): Used for Saves requiring physical power, like lifting gates, bending bars, and resisting poison. STR is damaged by wounds, scrapes, cuts, or bruises. Your Character is dead if their STR drops to 0. | Dexterity (DEX): Used for Saves requiring poise, speed, and reflexes, such as dodging, climbing, sneaking, or balancing. DEX is damaged by sprains, cramps, poisons, and shocks. Your Character is paralyzed if their DEX drops to 0. | Willpower (WIL): Used for Saves requiring confidence, discipline, leadership, and connection to the spiritual and arcane power of the world. Examples include provoking, deceiving, or manipulating spells. WIL is damaged by fear, psychic attacks, low morale, and extreme mental strain. Your Character is mentally broken if their WIL drops to 0. | . Attributes are not universal descripters of a Character’s abilities. A Character with low STR is not necessarily hopelessly weak, but rather in risky, tense situations their strength is more likely to fail them.

102.title: Hit Protection (HP)
102.content: Hit Protection (HP) reflects an ability to avoid serious damage in combat. | This measurement does not indicate a Character’s health or fortitude, nor does it represent lasting damage to a Character, as it is relatively easy to recover. See Healing & Recovery and Damage, Injury, and Death. | Hit Protection only applies in combat. If a PC takes damage outside of combat, it should be taken from an Attribute, typically STR. | .

103.title: Resolution Systems
103.content: Saves . A Save is a roll to avoid negative outcomes thrust upon the players outside of their volition. Saves are typically used for moments of split-second decisions, when something suddenly happens to a Character. | Characters roll a d20 and compare the results to the appropriate attribute. If they roll equal to or under that attribute, they succeed. Otherwise, they fail. A 1 is always a success, and a 20 is always a failure. | If two opponents are each trying to overcome the other, whoever is most at risk should save. In the case this cannot be decided, make a Contested Roll. Both Characters make a Save in a relevant attribute, and the highest successful roll wins. In the case that both rolls fail, the situation gets worse for both participants. | If two Characters are affected by a Save, whoever is most at risk should save (usually the Character with the lowest relevant Attribute). | . Time, Gear, & Skill . When a Character consciously performs an action that is neither impossible, nor without chance of failure, use the Time, Gear & Skill system: . | If a Character is not explicitly in a rush, and they have time to calmly assess a situation and pick a best course of action, then they have Time. | If a Character has gear, equipment, or environmental benefits that outweigh the hindrences from such, then they have Gear. | If a Character has explicit skills or specialization in a task, then they have Skill. | . If a Character has neither Time, Gear, or Skill, they automatically fail at the Action. If a Character has Time, Gear, and Skill, then they automatically succeed in an action. If a Character has two of the three, they roll d6, and if they have one of the three, they roll d6 with Disadvantage: . | On a 1, they fail at a severe cost, and the situation changes. | On a 2-3, they succeed at a severe cost (e.g Fatigue and damage to self or item). | On a 4-6 they succeed. | . Consider the following examples of how a Character may have Time, Gear, and Skill: . When Lockpicking, a Character has Time if they have ten minutes to inspect the lock and fiddle with it, Gear if they have lockpicks, and Skill if they are a thief. When Sneaking, a Character has Time if they’re not being actively chased, Gear if they’re wearing soft-soled boots (and no heavy armor, or are carrying less than 5 items), and Skill if they’re trained to be stealthy. When Smashing a Door Open, a Character has Time if they’re not actively under attack, Gear if they have an axe, or a crowbar, and Skill if they were once a firefighter. When Climbing a Wall, a Character has Time if they’re not being chased, Gear if they’ve got a rope and pitons (and no heavy armor), and Skill if they were once an Acrobat. When Plotting an escape route through the city, a party have Time if they’re planning it well in advance, Gear if they have pencil and paper to note down guard routes, and good connection with the locals, and Skill if they’re a thief, or they have good familiarity with the city. The Die of Fate . When a Player asks a question that their Character would be able to determine the answer to, or when the fiction demands an answer, the GM first consults their prepared material. If that material, or the current state of the Fictional World, suggests a likely answer, the GM should use this to answer the Players question. In other situations, the GM may answer in one or two ways: . | If the answer does not significantly affect the situation, GMs should simply invent an answer, perhaps collaboratively with other Players. The answer may be evocative, or set the mood, but it should not substantially harm or help the Players. | If the answer significantly affects the situation, GMs may invoke the Die of Fate. The GM asks the Player the following two questions: . | What do they hope the answer to the question is. | What do they most fear the answer to the question is. | . GMs are motivated to add to the feared result if they feel higher stakes are necessary. The GM then rolls a d6. On a 1-3, what the Player most fears comes true. On a 4-6, what the Player hopes is true. | .

104.title: Inventory
104.content: A characters Inventory represents both their literal equipment load, as well as a kind of stamina bar, through the Conditions system. | Inventory is abstract, dependent only on the fiction as adjudicated by the GM. | Anyone filling 10 or more Inventory Slots is Encumbered, which means they cannot run, and are reduced to 0 HP. | Anyone carrying at most five items has a Light Load, and gains Advantage on all Saves which may be advantaged by being more light on their feet. | Horses, mules, carts, and hirelings can make a huge difference in how much a PC can bring with them on an adventure, though these options may bring with them their own problems. | As a group, your crew may always assume they are carrying torches, rope, and camping equipment, and these items do not take up Inventory Slots, unless the lack of these items becomes important to the fiction. Any other items should be explicitly written in your inventory, taking up Inventory Slots if they are not Petty. | . Inventory Slots . | Most items take up one slot unless otherwise indicated. | Petty items do not take up any slots. Bulky items take up two slots. | A bag of coins worth less than £100 is Petty. | . Conditions, Deprivation, and Fatigue . | Conditions are negative effects suffed by your character. Each Condition must be placed in an Inventory Slot, which now cannot be used to store any items. | Some Conditions have additional effects, which apply as long as the Condition is in your inventory. | Conditions have a requirement to be cleared from an inventory slot. | The most common Condition is Fatigue, cleared after the PC is able to recuperate (i.e. after a full night’s rest in a safe spot). | A PC that lacks a crucial need (such as food or rest) is Deprived. Anyone Deprived for more than a day adds Fatigue, one for each day. A Deprived PC cannot recover HP, cannot recover Attributes, and cannot clear Fatigue until they get rid of their Deprived status. | A PC may be forced to add Fatigue after casting spells or due to events in the fiction. | .

105.title: Damage, Injury and Death
105.content: In their adventures, Characters may be damaged in several ways: . | If a Character is damaged in combat, and the source of damage can be reasonably defended against, damage is taken from HP first, then a relevant Attribute, usually STR. See the Combat rules for more. | If a Character is damaged outside of combat, or the source of damage cannot be reasonably defended against (e.g. damage from a fall or from running through fire), the damage is done directly to an Attribute. | . When damage is done to an Attribute, determine whether this damage could potentially be lethal, or enough to take the Character out of action. If so, the Player rolls a Critical Damage Save in the damaged Attribute after taking damage. If the Character fails the Critical Damage Save, the Character is mortally wounded, and cannot do anything but crawl weakly, grasping for life. | If given relevant aid (typically bandages) they will stabilize, bearing a Severe Wound representing their critical damage. | If left untreated, they die within the hour. NPCs and monsters that fail a Critical Damage Save are considered dead, per the GM’s discretion. | . In addition, in Combat some enemies will have special abilities or effects that are triggered when their Target fails a Critical Damage Save. Some examples of damage: . Falling from a great height onto a hard surface does d10 STR damage per 10’ fallen, and requires a Critical Damage Save. Inhaling a certain noxious gas deals 1 STR damage per turn, with no Critical Damage Save required. Being exposed to frigid temperatures without proper clothing deals 1 STR and 1 DEX damage per Watch, with no Critical Damage Save required. Being injected with Night Spider venom deals d8 DEX damage, and requires a Critical Damage Save. Catching a glimpse of the unnatural deals 1 WIL damage, and does not require a Critical Damage Save. Gazing upon the true form of an eldritch being deals d8 WIL damage, and requires a Critical Damage Save. The following table can be used to determine the location of Critical Damage, adding flavor, though keep the fiction in mind: . |   |   | . | d10 | Location | . | 1-5 | Torso | . | 6 | Left Leg | . | 7 | Right Leg | . | 8 | Left Arm | . | 9 | Right Arm | . | 10 | Head | . Wounds . Some types of damage can leave Wounds, which hinder affected adventurers more than simply dealing damage. Fire can inflict burns, electricity can inflict nerve damage, cold can inflict frostbite, monsters can inflict wounds upon dealing Critical Damage, etc. A Wound is a Condition occupying a slot in the adventurer’s inventory. Wounds come in 3 levels. Whenever a wound of the same type is gained, the player can choose to have their existing wound increase in severity or to fill in a new inventory slot. This allows player choice between short or long-term impact: do you suffer another light wound and heal after combat or have the old wound increase in severity without taking up another Inventory slot but possibly leading to permanent injury? . | Light Wounds can be healed in the field if proper medicine and treatment are available. Light burns can be healed by gathering the right herbs and applying them overnight. | Severe Wounds require serious medical attention. Characters with Severe Wounds are considered Deprived until the wound is treated. Severe frostbite could be cured with a comfortable week-long rest in a healing temple. | Permanent Wounds can never be healed. Every Permanent Wound also lowers a fitting Attribute by -1 (For instance, WIL for nerve damage). While this -1 penalty can be overcome in time by training, the wound’s Inventory slot is forever lost. | . Character Death . When a character dies, the player should create a new Character or take control of a friendly NPC travelling with the party. They immediately join the party in order to reduce time sat out of the game.

106.title: Healing & Recovery
106.content: Catching Your Breathe . A Short Rest takes a few minutes, and consists of taking time to sip water and converse with your crew. A Short Rest fully restores lost HP, but may leave the party exposed. | Water is kept in a Waterskin, which occupies 1 slot. Fully filled it has a Usage Die of d8. Whenever a character recovers HP, they roll the Waterskin’s Usage Die. On a 1 or 2, the die decreases in size (from a d8, to a d6, to a d4). If a 1-2 is rolled on a d4 Usage Die, the waterskin is empty. | During a rest, players are encouraged to role-play a scene that develops the fellowship between the party. Share a memory, talk about goals, ask about their history or tell them yours. | . Long Term Recovery . | Bandages can stabilize a character that has taken Critical Damage, though the character will sustain Wounds. | Attribute Loss can be restored by a week’s rest in a comfortable spot. Recovery can be hastened by a healer or other appropriate source of expertise. | Some healing services are free, while magical or more expedient means of recovery may come at a cost. | .

107.title: Magic
107.content: Spellbooks . | Spellbooks contain a single spell and take up one Inventory Slot. They cannot be easily transcribed or created; instead they are recovered from places like tombs, dungeons, and manors. | Spellbooks sometimes display unusual properties or limitations, such as producing a foul or unearthly smell when opened, possessing an innate intelligence, or being legible only when held in moonlight. | Spellbooks will attract the attention of those who seek the arcane power within, and it is considered dangerous to display them openly. | . Casting Spells . | Anyone can cast a spell by holding a Spellbook in both hands and reading its contents aloud. They must then take a Fatigue. | Given time and safety, PCs can enhance a spell’s impact (affecting multiple targets, increasing its power, etc.) without additional cost. | If the PC is Deprived or in danger (such as during combat), the GM may require a PC to make a WIL save to avoid any ill-effects from casting the spell. Consequences of failure are on par with the intended effect and may result in additional Fatigue, the destruction of the Spellbook, injury, and even death. | .

108.title: Combat
108.content: Rounds . | Chime typically takes place without strict time accounting. In a fight, we use Rounds to keep track of when things occur, which is a unit of time roughly equal to ten seconds of in-game time. The main purpose of Rounds is to make sure all participants in combat take a turn. | Each Round starts with any PC that is able to act, followed by their opponents. All players declare their actions before being simultaneously resolved. Typically, the GM will telegraph the actions of opponents to some degree when prompted. | During the first Round of combat, each PC must make a DEX Save in order to act. Special circumstances, abilities, items, or skills may negate this requirement. PCs that fail their save lose their Turn for this round. | After the PC’s first round, their opponents then take their turn, and the first round ends. The next round begins with the PCs taking their turn, followed by their opponents, and so on until combat has ended with one side defeated or fled. | . Actions . On their Turn, a Character may move, and take one Action. This Action can be anything reasonably done in 10 seconds, such as casting a spell, attacking, or moving further. Each round, all PCs declare what they are doing before dice are rolled. If a character attempts something risky, the GM calls for an appropriate resolution system. Attacking & Damage . Attacks in combat automatically hit, following the procedure below when the attack would feasibly damage an opponent: . | All attackers of a target roll their damage die, keeping only the highest roll. | The target’s AP is subtracted from the result. | The remaining damage is dealt to the target’s HP. | If HP is reduced to 0, leftover damage is dealt to STR. | Direct damage to STR triggers a Critical Damage Save. | . | Attacks that damage HP but not STR can be narrated as leaving the target off-balance, dazed, staggered, shaken, or grazed. Only damage to STR leaves meaningful wounds. | If an attack would take a PC’s HP exactly to 0, refer to the Scars table to see how they are uniquely impacted. | Unaware targets are unable to protect themselves, meaning damage is done directly to STR. Similarily, Encumbered characters are overburdened to the degree that they cannot protect themselves. | . Attack Modifiers . | If fighting from a position of weakness (such as through cover or with bound hands), the attack is Impaired, and the attacker must roll d4 damage regardless of their damage die. Unarmed attacks always do d4 damage. | If fighting from a position of advantage, such as through a daring, dramatic maneuver, the attack is Enhanced, allowing the attacker to roll d12 damage instead of their normal die. | Daring maneuvers often come with additional risk. Perhaps the move will leave the character vulnerable, making the first incoming attack on the next turn Enhanced as well. Alternatively, the move might require a save at the risk of Fatigue, STR damage, or damaged or lost gear. Make sure you are clear about the consequences of your action before committing to carrying the action out. | . | Attacks with the Blast quality damage all targets in the noted area, rolling separately for each affected character. This can be anything from explosions to a dragon’s breath or the impact of a meteorite. If unsure how many targets can be affected, roll the related damage die for a result. | If attacking with two weapons at the same time, roll both damage dice and keep the single highest result (denoted with a plus symbol, e.g. d8+d8). | . Gambits . | When making an attack, a character can make a Gambit instead. A gambit is a move such as stunning, shoving, disarming, tripping, and so forth. Gambits deal no damage. | To perform a gambit, the Attacker rolls their weapon’s damage die. If this roll comes up on a 1, 2, or 3, this becomes the Difficulty of the Save. On a 4 or higher, no Difficulty is applied. The target makes a relevant Save, as determined by the DM, rolling equal to or below the Attribute but above the Difficulty. | If the Target fails their Save, the Gambit succeeds. If the Gambit fails, the Target may immediately try a gambit of their own against the Attacker. This effect causes a chain of failed gambits until one succeeds. | If attackers choose to deal damage to the same target as a gambit, resolve gambits before rolling for damage. If more than one gambit is taken against a single target, determine order based on which gambit would happen in the fiction first. | . Armor Protection (AP) . | Before calculating HP Damage, subtract the target’s Armor Protection (AP) value from the result. | Shields and similar armor provide a bonus to AP while worn. Some may also provide additional benefits, depending on the fiction. | No Character, PC or otherwise, can have more than 3 AP. | . Detachments . | Large groups of similar combatants fighting together are treated as a single Detachment. | Attacks against Detachments by individuals are Impaired (excluding Blast damage). Attacks against individuals by detachments are Enhanced and deal Blast damage. | Consider making a Critical Damage Save WIL rather than STR when it makes sense within the fiction, to represent routing large detachments rather than the much more difficult task of utterly destroying them. | . Retreat . | Running away from a dire situation, such as combat, always requires a successful DEX Save, as well as a safe destination to run to. | . Morale . | Enemies must pass a WIL Save to avoid fleeing when they take their first casualty and again when they lose half their number. Lone foes must save when they’re reduced to 0 HP. | Some groups may use their leader’s WIL in place of their own. | Morale does not affect PCs. | . Ranged Attacks . | Ranged weapons can target any enemy near enough to see the whites of their eyes. Attacks against especially distant targets are Impaired. | Ammunition is not tracked unless otherwise specified. | . Scars . If damage to a PC would reduce their HP to exactly 0, look up the result on the table below based on the amount of HP lost in the attack. For example, if a PC went from 3 HP to 0 HP, they would look at entry 3 (Walloped). |   |   | . | **HP Lost** | Result | . | 1 | Lasting Scar: Roll 1d6. 1: Neck, 2: Hands, 3: Eye, 4: Chest, 5: Legs, 6: Ear. Roll 1d6. If the total is higher than your max HP, take the new result. | . | 2 | Rattling Blow: You’re disoriented and shaken. Describe how you refocus. Roll 1d6. If the total is higher than your max HP, take the new result. | . | 3 | Walloped: You’re sent flying and land flat on your face, winded. You are Deprived until you rest for a few hours. Then, roll 1d6. Add that amount to your max HP. | . | 4 | Broken Limb: Roll 1d6. 1-2: Leg, 3-4: Arm, 5: Rib, 6: Skull. Once mended, roll 2d6. If the total is higher than your max HP, take the new result. | . | 5 | Diseased: You’re afflicted with a gross, uncomfortable infection. When you get over it, roll 2d6. If the total is higher than your max HP, take the new result. | . | 6 | Reorienting Head Wound: Roll 1d6. 1-2: STR, 3-4: DEX, 5-6: WIL. Roll 3d6. If the total is higher than your current Attribute, take the new result. | . | 7 | Hamstrung: You can barely move until you get serious help and rest. After recovery, roll 3d6. If the total is higher than your max DEX, take the new result. | . | 8 | Deafened: You cannot hear anything until you find extraordinary aid. Regardless, make a WIL save. If you pass, increase your max WIL by 1d4. | . | 9 | Re-brained: Some hidden part of your psyche is knocked loose. Roll 3d6. If the total is higher than your max WIL, take the new result. | . | 10 | Sundered: An appendage is torn off, crippled, or useless. (The GM will tell you which). Then make a WIL save. If you pass, increase your max WIL by 1d6. | . | 11 | Mortal Wound: You are Deprived and out of action. You die in one hour unless healed. Upon recovery, roll 2d6. Take the new result as your max HP. | . | 12 | Doomed: Death seemed ever so close, but somehow you survived. If your next save against Critical Damage is a fail, you die horribly. If you pass, roll 3d6. If the total is higher than your max HP, take the new result. | .

109.title: Corpse Collector
109.content: More of a Corpse Transporter. But the other name made it easier for people to despise you.

112.title: What’s The Last Thing You Found On A Corpse?
112.content: |   |   | . | £2 | Corpse Warts, now on your face. | . | £4 | A Crawling Hand (1HP) that follows basic instructions with something of a bad attitude. | . | £6 | An Explosive Cigar (d8). | . | £8 | A Beautiful Silk Scarf, the smell is mostly gone. | . | £10 | A one-shot Duelling Pistol (d8, needs a Rest to reload). | . | £12 | A Repeating Doll (can record a single phrase, repeats when you tickle it). | .

113.title: What Was Your Job On The Side?
113.content: |   |   | . | 1HP | Plumber: Take a Set of Spanners. | . | 2HP | Window Cleaner: Take a Collapsible Ladder and Bucket. | . | 3HP | Bleacher: Take a Bottle of Bleach. | . | 4HP | Paper Seller: Take a Bike Horn. | . | 5HP | Unclogger: Soup dispensers clog up easily, so you fixed them. Take a Plunger. | . | 6HP | Tile Fixer: Take a Tub of Grout. | .

114.title: Council Runner
114.content: With so many councils, a runner is a position of some power. Now your time is finished.

117.title: What’s The Best Thing You Learned During Your Time As A Runner?
117.content: |   |   | . | £2 | Secret to Drinking Well: You can out-drink anyone. | . | £4 | The Quickest Way To The Underground from any point in The City. | . | £6 | Skip-Stride Technique. It looks ridiculous, but when you use it you can easily outrun the others in your group. | . | £8 | Courier-Throwing. You never miss a catch, or make a bad throw. | . | £10 | A few Code Words that get you a free ride on roughly half of The City’s transport enterprises. | . | £12 | The Intimate Nickname of a powerful local politician. | .

118.title: What Did You Steal?

176.title: The Basics
176.content: A Dungeon is any dangerous locale, including, but not limited to, actual dungeons. | The Dungeon exploration cycle (see below) is divided into a series of Turns, Actions, and their consequences. | On their Turn, a Character can move a distance equal to their torchlight’s perimeter (about 40ft), and perform one Action. Players can use their Action to move up to three times that distance though that will increase the chance of triggering a roll on the Dungeon Events table. | The GM should present obvious information about an area and its dangers freely and at no cost. Moving quickly or without caution may increase the chance of encountering a wandering monster, springing a trap, or triggering a roll on the Dungeon Events table. | .

177.title: Dungeon Exploration Cycle
177.content: . | The GM describes the party’s surroundings and any immediate dangers (combat, traps, surprises, etc.). The players then declare their character’s intended movements and Actions. | The GM resolves the Actions of each character simultaneously, along with any Actions that are already in progress. | The Players record any loss of resources and any new conditions (e.g item use or Deprivation). The cycle then begins again. If appropriate, the GM should roll on the Dungeon Events table. Keep common sense in mind when interpreting the results! | .

178.title: Dungeon Events
178.content: Exploring a dungeon is always dangerous, and time must always be weighed against the risk of awakening the location’s denizens, natural hazards, and worse. When the party: . | Spends more than one dungeon cycle in a single room or location | Moves quickly or haphazardly through a room | Moves into a new area, level, or zone | Creates a loud disturbance | . Roll on the table below. |   |   |   | . | 1 | Encounter | Roll on an encounter table. Possibly hostile. (See Reactions.) | . | 2 | Sign | A clue, spoor, track, abandoned lair, scent, victim, etc is discovered. | . | 3 | Environment | Surroundings shift or escalate. Water rises, ceilings collapse, a ritual nears completion, etc. | . | 4 | Loss | Torches are blown out, an ongoing spell fizzles, etc. The party must resolve the effect before moving on. | . | 5 | Exhaustion | The party must rest (triggering another roll on this table), add a Fatigue, or consume a ration. | . | 6 | Quiet | The party is left alone (and safe) for the time being. | .

179.title: Actions
179.content: . | Actions are any non-passive activities, such as searching for traps, forcing open a door, listening for danger, disarming a trap, engaging an enemy in combat, casting a spell, dodging a trap, running away, resting, etc. | Some Actions have special rules (see below), while others may take multiple Turns to complete. | Loud or noticeable Actions may also trigger an Encounter with the dungeon’s denizens. | . Searching . | A character can spend a Turn performing an exhaustive search of one object or location in an area, revealing any relevant hidden treasure, traps, secret doors, etc. | Larger rooms and difficult or complex dungeon terrain may take a few Turns to properly search. | Searching a room first is a safer way to explore the dungeon, but it has a steep cost: time. | .

180.title: Panic
180.content: . | A character that is surrounded by enemies, enveloped by darkness, or facing their greatest fears may experience panic. A WIL save is typically required to avoid losing control and becoming panicked. | A panicked character must make a WIL save to overcome their condition as an Action on their Turn. | A panicked character has 0 HP, does not act in the first round of combat, and all of their attacks are Impaired. | .

181.title: Dungeon Elements
181.content: Light . | Torches and other radial sources of light illuminate 40ft of dungeon and beyond that only a dim outline of objects. Torches last until they are put out by a character or their environment. | Characters without a light source may suffer from panic until their situation is remedied. | . Doors . | Doors and entryways may be locked, stuck, or blocked entirely. Characters can try to force a door open (or wedge it shut) using available resources (spikes, glue) or through raw ability. | The party’s marching order determines who is most impacted by whatever lies beyond a door. | A character can detect, through careful observation (listening, smelling, etc.), signs of life and other hazards through nearby doors and walls. | . Traps . | A cautious character should be presented with any and all information that would allow them the opportunity to avoid springing a trap. An unwitting character will trigger a trap according to the fiction, or otherwise will have a 2-in-6 chance. | Traps can usually be detected by carefully searching a room. | Damage from traps is taken from Attributes (usually STR or DEX) and not from HP. Armor can reduce damage, but only if applicable (e.g. a shield would not reduce damage from noxious gas). | .

258.title: Home
258.content: A year ago they took you. Black bagged, condemned to a cell. It wasn’t fair. It didn’t matter. Six months passed, and a woman immaculate and dangerous as a polished shiv offered you a deal. The Martel Company needs recruits. The work will be hard. Dangerous. But you can buy back your name. In time. For six months you hauled rocks. Big stones sunk deep in the mud. Knotburns buried deep into your palms. You were not alone. They put you on a ship, and the crew became your world. You slept together, bathed together, ate together, shat together. Maybe you’ve become friends. Maybe you hate each others guts. But you trust them, because you must. They branded your left shoulder when the ship set sail. Black magic swirled around the iron, felt like ice when you were marked. They told you that you belong to the Martel now, and that your job is to haul. Gold. Statues. Art. The more you haul, the quicker your brand will fade. You’re free to run. But in time, the brand will consume your flesh, sloughed off like corn husk. And so with your crew, they sailed you to the Swordfish Islands… . Chime is an adventure game about exploring a dark and mysterious island of primal forces, life, and abundance, mainly a hack of Cairn, divined by the Haruspex of Hotsprings Island from the entrails of: . | Block, Dodge, Parry! | Electric Bastionland. | His Majesty the Worm. | . Character generation is quick and random, adventures are tense and reward careful exploration, and combat is frantic and deadly.

Take a Gold Tooth (worth £500, not easy to remove) and Bottle of Expensive Perfume. | . | 3HP | After foiling a plot to blow up your lock for insurance purposes, You Were Dismissed From Duty. Take a Bomb (d10 Blast). | . | 4HP | Canal Pirates Wiped Out Your Organisation. Take a Bloody Cutlass (d6). | . | 5HP | You were sent out on a supply run, and Came Back To A Ruined Lock. 449.title: Ally
449.content: You are professional and amiable to your crew-mate. This is probably the default archetype. Examples: . | Wash and Kaylee. | Aragorn and Gandalf. | .

450.title: Adversary
450.content: If you have a significant problem with a crew-mate, you’re probably their adversary. This isn’t an excuse to be an asshole to other Players in the game, but it’s an opportunity for interesting and fair conflict within the game. Examples: . | Mal and Jayne. | Boromir and Aragorn. | .

451.title: Best Friend
451.content: You wouldn’t let your best friend go on an adventure without you. You’re there for each other, through thick and thin. Examples: . | Mal and Zoe. | Merry and Pippin. | .

452.title: Love
452.content: If both players are cool with it, two characters may be romantically interested in one another. If the relationship is one sided, Unrequited Love may be more applicable. Examples: . | Zoe and Wash. | Faramir and Éowyn. | .

453.title: Master / Henchmen
453.content: When you have a managerial vs. subservient relationship with another crew-mate, you probably fit a Master / Henchmen archetype. Examples: . | Mal and Wash. | Frodo and Sam. | .

454.title: Mentor / Mentee
454.content: When a parent, parent-figure, or mentor guies and shapes the life of a mentee, they fit the Mentor / Mentee archetype. Examples: . | Shepherd Book and River. | Gandalf and Frodo. | .

455.title: Rival
455.content: You are in competition with your rival. You might want the same woman, the most kills, or something weirder. Examples: . | Legolas and Gimli. | .

456.title: Sibling
456.content: Your first adventures were raiding your mother’s cookie jar together. Now you’re crew-mates. Examples: . | Simon and River. | Boromir and Faramir. | .

457.title: Unrequited Love
457.content: He’s just not that into you. If both players are cool with it, use this archetype to represent how you feel about a would-be suitor or ex-lover. Examples: . | Simon and Kaylee. | Aragorn and Éowyn. | .

555.title: The City
555.content: . | It’s the only city that matters. All others have failed. Too big to fall, too complicated to function. A mess of alleyways, legalese and crowds. You can find everything there. Nobody knows who’s in charge. | Modern things exist, but in old-fashioned, turn of the century style. Technology is unevenly distributed. If something can be made electric, somebody is doing it. | The City is populated by both Man and Mockery, animal creatures of felt, wood, and string given life-spark. They act as if they have human needs, but it is all imitated. It has been generations since they arrived, and now no-one bats an eye to their presence. They behave as if they’re on stage, addressing an imagined audience. At some times their sense of theater can border on precognition or omniscience. | Deep Country consists of the remnants of the old places, before The City became dominant. There are cities out there, but none of them reached Modernity. Few in The City cares what happens out there. Everyone in Deep Country has a history, but nobody is concerned about the future. They decline while The City grows. | The Underground sits just beneath reality, connecting The City to everything, and everything bleeds back into the Underground. It seems man-made, but warped, purposes exaggerated or inverted. There are Machines here, one with The Underground. They want to test you. | Several philosophies have become predominant as of late in The City: . | Spiritualists gaze to The Living Stars. They idolize what might live there, but there are infinite choices of who and what to worship up there. | Machinists seek shortcuts from The Machines in The Underground. | Humanists think humanity should be on top, whether romanticizing the simplicity of the past, or fetishizing modernity. | . | .

556.title: Wilderness Travel
556.content: . In this campaign, you’ll be navigating Hot Springs Island, which is made up of twenty-five Hexes, each 2 miles in diameter. Each hex contains 3 points of interest to discover and explore. These points are all physical locations that can be revisited, rather than one-time events or encounters. The Hot Springs Field Guide contains a log to keep track of which locations you’ve visited.

557.title: Time
557.content: In order to abstract time to the scale of overland travel, we divide a day into six 4-hour units of time, called Watches. Days are divided up into six 4-hour Watches (Dawn, Day, Day, Dusk, Night, Night). At Dawn on each day, the Weather is rolled. Then the Party successively decides how they want to spend each Watch in the day. Each of the following Actions takes 1 Watch: Navigation, Investigation, Regrouping. Weather . At the start of each day, the GM rolls for Weather. They roll a d6: . | On a 1-3, the weather is Favorable - clear skies and a slight breeze. | On a 4-5, the weather is Unfavorable - gusting winds, rain, sweltering heat, or sudden chills. Travel between Hexes takes either 2 Watches, or 1 Watch and each player in the Party takes one Fatigue. | On a 6, the weather is Terrible. Examples include thunderstorms, heavy rain, flash flooding or mudslides. Travel between Hexes takes 2 Watches and each Player in the Party takes one Fatigue. | If a 6 is rolled two days in a row, the weather becomes Catastrophic. Examples include Hurricanes, Blood Rain, or Ash Clouds. Travel becomes practically impossible. | . The dynamics of Time, Gear, and Skill apply to weather effects. Dedicated Gear relevant to the bad weather (e.g climbing equipment in mountains, or rain hoods during mild rain) or Skill relevant to the bad weather (e.g training in navigation or mountaineering) can mitigate the negative effects of bad weather. Navigation and Investigation of Hexes . Each Hex is populated with difficult to navigate terrain, such as mountains, jungle, and woods. The Party takes one Watch to either Navigate to an adjacent Hex, or to Investigate the current Hex to find new locations. After performing these actions, an Encounter is then determined, which occurs at some point of the Party’s journey. Then the Party arrives at a location, and may spend the rest of a Watch exploring that location. Navigation . To Navigate to an adjacent Hex, the Party chooses an adjacent Hex and, if known, a location within that Hex to travel to, then makes a Time, Gear & Skill Check (the Party has Time if they have marked the location via a Check Maps Action during Regrouping, Gear if they have equipment that aids in Navigation like a Compass, and Skill if they have already discovered the location they are travelling to, though gaining these by other measures is also possible). If the Party Succeeds, they arrive at their planned end destination. If they Fail, roll a d6: . | On a 1-3, the Party ends up back where they started. | On a 4, the Party veers left. | On a 5, the Party veers right. | On a 6, the Party arrives at their destination by sheer luck. | . Veering into a Hex populated by ocean results in the Party finding themselves on a nondescript stretch of beach. Investigation . To Investigate a Hex the Party currently occupies, the Party spends a Watch, then makes a Time, Gear, & Skill Check as with Navigation. On a Success, the Party discovers a new location within the Hex. On a Failure, use a d6 to determine a surrounding Hex that the party veers into. If possible, the Party travels to the adjacent Hex, potentially discovering a new location there instead. Regrouping . Regrouping takes one Watch and allows each Character to individually choose from a variety of actions: . Check Maps . Choose a Hex on the Map and mark it. For the next 24 hours, when Navigating or Investigating this Hex, you will have Time on your Time, Gear, & Skill Check to avoid getting lost. Forecast . Use magic or experience to forecast the weather. The GM rolls a d6: on a 1-2, they roll a random forecast on the weather table. On a 3-4, they roll tomorrow’s weather early, and relay this information. On a 5-6, they roll the day after next’s weather early, and relay this information. Boost Morale . Share a riveting tale, sing a song, or otherwise inspire. Everyone recovers 1 WIL, or 2 WIL if the performer has any relevant skills or background. Cook Food . Prepare food from gathered ingredients. Simple Meals require a fire, and Fancy Meals require a fire and Cooking Pot. Gathering . There are several types of Gathering: . | Hunting involves killing butchering animals that are risky to kill, and requires tools like bows or spears, and yields Raw Meat. You may come across more dangerous animals during Navigation and Investigation Encounters, which may yield more meat for less time investment, but comes at the risk of harm. | Foraging requires no tools and yields Fruit and Vegetables, and in addition, has a 1 in 6 chance of finding Wildcrafting materials. | Fishing requires tools like fishing rods or spears and yields Raw Fish. | . Yield depends on the terrain (quantified in # of Portions of food). Character explicitly skilled in an activity may upgrade the die roll by one tier, i.e. from 1 to d4 to d6 to d8. Up to three portions of any one ingredient may occupy an inventory slot. |   |   |   |   | . | Biome | Hunting | Foraging | Fishing | . | Village | 1 | 1 | d4 | . | Ruin | 1 | - | - | . | Mountainous Jungle | 1 | d4 | 1 | . | Volcano | 1 | - | - | . | Volcanic | 1 | 1 | - | . | Heavy Jungle | d4 | d6 | - | . | Light Jungle | d6 | d4 | 1 | . | Beach | d4 | 1 | d6 | . Wildcrafting . Wildcrafting allows one to craft healing salves, teas, and tonics, with ingredients found in the wild. Ingredients can be found while foraging and consist of mushrooms, mosses, plants, roots, bark, and saps. An ingredient’s rarity determines it’s Potency (1 for Common, 2 for Uncommon,and 4 for Rare). Furthermore, ingredients are defined by the type of region they are gathered from. Each recipe has a Potency that must be met by combining ingredients. Each recipe must contain at least 2 different ingredients, and some recipes have requirements for region. Recipes may be learned from sages, and other wood-wise types. Camping, Eating, and Sleeping . The Party may decide to set up camp for the day, choosing a number of Watches to camp for. Characters who camp for 2 Watches recover Fatigue. The Party may choose to take a free Regrouping Action, but this generates noise, and may bring undesirable attention to the Party’s camp. Characters must sleep for at least 1 Watch, or become Deprived of sleep. Characters who only camp for 1 Watch are not Deprived, but do not recover Fatigue from sleep. Characters must also must eat at least one portion of food a day to avoid becoming Deprived of food: . | Desperate Meals are only eaten when in dire need of food, such as raw meat and fish, snakes, treefrogs, rats, and so on. WIL Save to not puke it up. | Fresh Meals consist of foraged fruit and vegetables that are otherwise not prepared. | Simple Meals are ingredients that have seen some basic level of preparation, such as grilled meat and roasted vegetables. Restores 1 Fatigue. Requires a fire. | Fancy Meals are made from multiple prepared ingredients put together, such as boiled vegetables with grilled fish, or boar meat sandwiches. This restores all Fatigue. Requires Fire and a Cooking Pot (Bulky). | .

624.title: What’s The One Creature You Couldn’t Kill? (They Now Accompany You)
624.content: |   |   | . | 1HP | Devourer. A football sized bug that curls up in your backpack. Can be woken once per day to eat its own size in organic material, no matter how inedible. | . | 2HP | Maggot Prince. A tiny maggot with a gold band marking. Flies and maggots vacate the area to make room for it, and anything that eats bugs is strongly drawn to try and eat it. | . | 3HP | Nightmare Moth Anyone but you sleeping in the same room as this moth suffers horrible nightmares. When they wake, the moth takes on the form of something from the nightmare. | . | 4HP | Terrormite. A big termite, pinned down in its box. Once released it burrows into the nearest living creature it can find, causing intense pain. Within a minute, the victim develops a tough outer skin (AP 1) and can spit acid (d6, Ongoing). This lasts for an hour until the victim coughs out d6 Terrormite Larva and returns to normal. | . | 5HP | Snake-Cat. A cloth toy with a snake body and cat head. If you’re confronted by a hostile snake, it transforms into a cat (4HP, d4 Claws). If you’re confronted by a hostile cat, it transforms into a snake (4HP, d6 Bite, d6 STR loss on CRITICAL DAMAGE). | . | 6HP | Large and Peaceful Dog. The only animal you can tolerate (6HP, d6 Bite in retaliation only). | .

625.title: Wall-Born
625.content: The City has rings of walls all the way to its centre, wherever that is. You were both warden and resident.

627.title: You Start With:

629.title: What Did You Find Behind a Loose Brick?
629.content: |   |   | . | 1HP | Beastmode Formula. A liquid tonic that grants the drinker the properties of a specific animal for one minute. Examples are: Eyes of a Hawk, Nose of a Dog, Strength of a Bear. Three doses. | . | 2HP | Orphan’s Ruin. 6oz of Milky Drink. 1oz pacifies any unease, 2oz causes a deep sleep, 3oz eternal sleep. | . | 3HP | Stem-Stone Pebble that transforms into whatever non-living material it last touched. Currently the leather of your pouch. | . | 4HP | Plague Wart. A carefully extracted wart sealed in a jar. Anyone that touches it contracts a disgusting plague, losing d6 STR every hour. The victim’s touch immediately spreads the plague. There is no cure that you know of. | . | 5HP | Jerk Sprite. A statuette of dried meat. Whatever eats this acts like an utter jerk for the next hour, even to their most loved ones. | . | 6HP | Universal Pin. A hairpin that sticks into any surface, no matter the material. Causes no pain if stuck into flesh. | .

630.title: Wilderness Recluse
630.content: Modernity gets between us and our natural instincts. You’re here begrudgingly after a lifetime in the wilds of Deep Country.

635.content: You’ve spent a long time in colleges, arguing about words. Your career prospects are poor.
